title_en: The Twenty-Five Types of Persons Classified by Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I have heard of the yin-yang types of persons — what are they?' Bogao replied: 'Within Heaven and Earth, within the six directions, nothing departs from the five phases, and human beings correspond to them as well. Hence the five phases, each divided fivefold, give rise to the twenty-five types of persons — but the yin-yang types of persons are not among them. Among these twenty-five, there are also five whose bearing does not conform to the ordinary pattern, and these I already understand. I wish to hear of the bodily forms of the twenty-five types, of how they arise from blood and qi, so that, distinguishing them, one may use them as signs — knowing the interior from the exterior. How is this done?' Qibo said: 'How thorough a question this is! This is a secret handed down by my former teachers, one which even Bogao himself is not able to fully explain.' Huangdi rose from his mat, stepped back respectfully, and said: 'I have heard that to find the right person and not teach him is called a grave loss; yet to obtain such knowledge and disclose it carelessly, Heaven itself would despise the act. I wish only to obtain it and understand it clearly, to store it away in a casket of gold, and not dare to make it known abroad.' Qibo said: 'One must first establish the five bodily forms — metal, wood, water, fire, and earth — distinguish their five colors, and differentiate the persons of these five forms; then the twenty-five types will all be accounted for.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo said: 'This must be handled with the utmost care — allow your servant to speak of it.'

The wood-type person corresponds to shangjiao (upper jiao), jiao being the note of the pentatonic scale associated with the wood phase, and resembles the Blue Sovereign, one of the five legendary rulers of antiquity correlated with the five phases. Such a person is blue-green in complexion, small-headed, long-faced, broad-shouldered and straight-backed, with a small trunk and shapely hands and feet. He is talented, taxes his mind but has little physical strength, and is much given to worry; he labors hard at his affairs. He thrives in spring and summer but cannot tolerate autumn and winter — when affected by these seasons, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Jueyin channel, and carries himself with a settled, dignified bearing. The da-jiao (great jiao) type corresponds to the left Foot Shaoyang channel, and above the Shaoyang moves with an easy, yielding gait. The zuo-jiao (left jiao) type corresponds to the right Foot Shaoyang channel, and below the Shaoyang moves with a following, compliant bearing. The dai-jiao (metal-tipped jiao) type corresponds to the right Foot Shaoyang channel, and above the Shaoyang moves with a pushing, forward-leaning bearing. The pan-jiao (split jiao) type corresponds to the left Foot Shaoyang channel, and below the Shaoyang carries a withered, gaunt bearing.

The fire-type person corresponds to shangzhi (upper zhi), the note associated with the fire phase, and resembles the Red Sovereign. Such a person is red in complexion, broad in the ridges of the back, narrow in the face, small-headed, with fine shoulders and back, a small pelvis and abdomen, and small hands and feet. He walks with his feet planted firmly yet his heart urgent, and as he walks his shoulders sway and his back is well fleshed. He is spirited but careless with money, has little constancy, and is much given to anxious deliberation; he sees matters clearly and has a fine countenance, but being impetuous of heart he does not live long and may die suddenly. He thrives in spring and summer but cannot tolerate autumn and winter — in autumn and winter, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Hand Shaoyin channel, and carries himself with a firm, compact bearing. The zhi-zhi (substantive zhi) type corresponds to the left Hand Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang has a full-fleshed bearing. The shao-zhi (lesser zhi) type corresponds to the right Hand Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang moves with a leisurely, unhurried bearing. The you-zhi (right zhi) type corresponds to the right Hand Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a sharp, angular bearing. The zhi-pan (substantive-split zhi) type corresponds to the left Hand Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang carries a full-cheeked, ample-jawed bearing.

The earth-type person corresponds to shanggong (upper gong), the note associated with the earth phase, and resembles the Yellow Sovereign of high antiquity. Such a person is yellow in complexion, round-faced, large-headed, with fine shoulders and back, a large abdomen, shapely thighs and shins, small hands and feet, and an ample build; the upper and lower body are well proportioned, he walks with his feet planted firmly, yet lifts his feet lightly as he steps. He is calm at heart, delights in benefiting others, has no fondness for power or position, and readily attaches himself to others. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Taiyin channel, and carries himself with a solid, substantial bearing. The da-gong (great gong) type corresponds to the left Foot Yangming channel, and above the Yangming moves with a graceful, yielding bearing. The jia-gong (added gong) type corresponds to the left Foot Yangming channel, and below the Yangming moves with a halting, uneven bearing. The shao-gong (lesser gong) type corresponds to the right Foot Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries a pivoting, well-hinged bearing. The zuo-gong (left gong) type corresponds to the right Foot Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a lofty, towering bearing.

The metal-type person corresponds to shangshang (upper shang), the note associated with the metal phase, and resembles the White Sovereign. Such a person has a square face, white complexion, a small head, small shoulders and back, a small abdomen, small hands and feet; his bones seem to protrude at the heels, and his skeletal frame is light. He is upright and incorruptible in character, urgent of heart yet composed and resolute, and makes a fine official. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Hand Taiyin channel, and carries himself with a solid, substantial bearing. The dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) type corresponds to the left Hand Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries an incisive, well-defined bearing. The you-shang (right shang) type corresponds to the left Hand Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a loose, unrestrained bearing. The zuo-shang (left shang) type corresponds to the right Hand Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries a watchful, scrutinizing bearing. The shao-shang (lesser shang) type corresponds to the right Hand Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a stern, severe bearing.

The water-type person corresponds to shangyu (upper yu), the note associated with the water phase, and resembles the Black Sovereign. Such a person is dark in complexion, with an uneven face, a large head, a narrow jaw, small shoulders, a large abdomen, and restless hands and feet; when he walks his body sways, his lower back and buttocks are long, and his back stretches out in a lengthy, drawn-out line. He shows little reverence or awe, is prone to deceiving and slighting others, and often meets a violent death. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Shaoyin channel, and carries himself with a moist, yielding bearing. The da-yu (great yu) type corresponds to the right Foot Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a full-cheeked bearing. The shao-yu (lesser yu) type corresponds to the left Foot Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang carries a clean, spare bearing. The zhong-yu (multitude yu) type corresponds to the right Foot Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang likewise carries a clean, spare bearing. The zhi-yu (fettered yu) type corresponds to the left Foot Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a settled, composed bearing. Thus the twenty-five variations among the five bodily-type persons are precisely what ordinary people confuse with one another.

Huangdi said: 'What of one who has the bodily form but not the corresponding color?' Qibo said: 'When the form overcomes the color, or the color overcomes the form, then, when the year arrives at which one phase overcomes the other, if the person is affected by pathogenic influence at that time, illness will follow; if he fails to take precautions, he will have cause for concern. When form and color correspond properly to one another, the person will enjoy wealth, honor, and great happiness.' Huangdi said: 'Can one know in advance the years at which form and color come to overcome one another?' Qibo said: 'In general, the years of taboo for all persons, above and below, follow a great rule: they recur every seven years — at sixteen, twenty-five, thirty-four, forty-three, fifty-two, and sixty-one years of age, these are all great taboo-years for a person, and one must take care to keep oneself at ease during them. If affected at such a time, illness will follow; if precautions are neglected, there will be cause for concern. At such times, one should refrain from reckless and unwholesome conduct — this is what is called the yearly taboo.'

Huangdi said: 'You have spoken of the upper and lower portions of the vessels, and of how the signs of blood and qi seem to reveal the state of one's bodily form — how does this work?' Qibo said: 'When blood and qi are abundant in the upper portion of the Foot Yangming channel, the beard grows fine and long; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the beard is short; when qi is scant and blood abundant, the beard is sparse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no beard at all, and the corners of the mouth show many fine lines. When blood and qi are abundant in the lower portion of the Foot Yangming channel, the pubic hair grows fine and reaches up to the chest; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the pubic hair is fine but shorter, reaching only to the navel, and in walking such a person tends to lift his feet high, with little flesh on the toes and feet that are prone to cold; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the feet are fleshy but prone to chilblains; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no hair at all, or if present it is sparse and withered, and such a person is prone to wilting weakness, reversal, and impediment of the feet.'

In the upper portion of the Foot Shaoyang channel, when qi and blood are abundant, the connecting whiskers linking the beard and sideburns grow fine and long; when blood is abundant and qi scant, they are fine but short; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the beard is sparse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no beard at all, and such a person, when affected by cold and dampness, is prone to impediment, with pain in the bones and withered nails. In the lower portion of the Foot Shaoyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the leg hair grows fine and long, and the outer ankle is full; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the leg hair is fine but short, and the skin of the outer ankle is firm and thick; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the leg hair is sparse, and the skin of the outer ankle is thin and soft; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no hair at all, and the outer ankle is thin and fleshless.

In the upper portion of the Foot Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the eyebrows are fine, with long fine hairs among them; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the eyebrows are coarse, and the face has fine, sparse lines; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the face is well fleshed; and when blood and qi are harmonious, the complexion is fine. In the lower portion of the Foot Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the heel is well fleshed and firm; when qi is scant and blood abundant, the heel is thin and hollow; and when both blood and qi are scant, such a person is prone to cramping of the sinews and pain beneath the heel.

In the upper portion of the Hand Yangming channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the mustache is fine; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the mustache is coarse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no mustache at all. In the lower portion of the Hand Yangming channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the underarm hair is fine, and the fleshy mound at the base of the thumb is warm; and when both qi and blood are scant, the hand is thin and cold.

In the upper portion of the Hand Shaoyin channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the eyebrows are fine and long, and the color of the ears is fine; when both blood and qi are scant, the ears are scorched-looking and of poor color. In the lower portion of the Hand Shaoyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the hand is well-rounded, fleshy, and warm; when both blood and qi are scant, the hand is cold and thin; and when qi is scant and blood abundant, the hand is thin with prominent vessels.

In the upper portion of the Hand Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the beard is thick, and the face is well fleshed and even; when both blood and qi are scant, the face is thin and of poor color. In the lower portion of the Hand Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the palm is full and fleshy; when both blood and qi are scant, the palm is thin and cold.

Huangdi said: 'For these twenty-five types of persons, is there a general rule for needling them?' Qibo said: 'One with fine eyebrows has abundant qi and blood in the Foot Taiyang channel; one with coarse eyebrows has scant qi and blood there. One who is stout and lustrous has a surplus of both qi and blood; one who is stout but without luster has a surplus of qi but a deficiency of blood; one who is thin and without luster has both qi and blood deficient. By carefully examining whether the bodily form and qi are in surplus or deficiency, and regulating accordingly, one can know whether the condition runs contrary to or in accord with what is proper.'

Huangdi said: 'How should one needle the various yin and yang channels?' Qibo said: 'Press the Cunkou and Renying pulses to gauge the balance of yin and yang, and palpate along the channels and network vessels for places of congelation and roughness, of binding and obstruction — wherever this occurs in the body, it manifests as pain and impediment, and in severe cases the flow ceases altogether; hence congelation and roughness arise. Where there is congelation and roughness, one should bring qi to the area to warm it, and only stop once the blood flows harmoniously. Where the network vessels are bound, the vessel is knotted and the blood disharmonious; one must open it before the flow can resume. Thus it is said: where qi has a surplus above, guide it downward; where qi is deficient above, press upon the area and let it rest; where qi lingers and fails to arrive, draw it forth by meeting it; one must be thoroughly versed in the pathways of the channels before one can properly control this. Where cold and heat contend with one another, guide the qi so that it flows freely. Where stagnant, old blood is present but not yet bound into a knot, press it out and disperse it. One must first thoroughly understand, for each of the twenty-five types of persons, where blood and qi are located, whether to the left or right, above or below — only then is the essential method of needling complete.'

title_en: The Five Notes and the Five Flavors
paragraphs_en: You-zhi (right zhi) and shao-zhi (lesser zhi): regulate the upper portion of the right Hand Taiyang channel. Zuo-shang (left shang) and zuo-zhi (left zhi): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. Shao-zhi (lesser zhi) and da-gong (great gong): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. You-jiao (right jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao): regulate the lower portion of the right Hand Shaoyang channel. Da-zhi (great zhi) and shao-zhi (lesser zhi): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Taiyang channel. Zhong-yu (multitude yu) and shao-yu (lesser yu): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Shao-shang (lesser shang) and you-shang (right shang): regulate the lower portion of the right Hand Taiyang channel. Zhi-yu (fettered yu) and zhong-yu (multitude yu): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Shao-gong (lesser gong) and da-gong (great gong): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Pan-jiao (split jiao) and shao-jiao (lesser jiao): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Shaoyang channel. Dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) and shang-shang (upper shang): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) and shang-jiao (upper jiao): regulate the lower portion of the left Foot Taiyang channel.

Shang-zhi (upper zhi) and you-zhi (right zhi) share the same grain, wheat; the same domestic animal, the sheep; and the same fruit, the apricot. They belong to the Hand Shaoyin channel and store in the heart; their color is red, their flavor bitter, and their season summer. Shang-yu (upper yu) and da-yu (great yu) share the same grain, the large bean; the same domestic animal, the pig; and the same fruit, the chestnut. They belong to the Foot Shaoyin channel and store in the kidney; their color is black, their flavor salty, and their season winter. Shang-gong (upper gong) and da-gong (great gong) share the same grain, panicled millet; the same domestic animal, the ox; and the same fruit, the jujube. They belong to the Foot Taiyin channel and store in the spleen; their color is yellow, their flavor sweet, and their season late summer. Shang-shang (upper shang) and you-shang (right shang) share the same grain, glutinous millet; the same domestic animal, the fowl; and the same fruit, the peach. They belong to the Hand Taiyin channel and store in the lung; their color is white, their flavor pungent, and their season autumn. Shang-jiao (upper jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the same grain, hemp; the same domestic animal, the dog; and the same fruit, the plum. They belong to the Foot Jueyin channel and store in the liver; their color is green-blue, their flavor sour, and their season spring.

Da-gong (great gong) and shang-jiao (upper jiao) share the same location, the upper portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Zuo-jiao (left jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the upper portion of the left Foot Yangming channel. Shao-yu (lesser yu) and da-yu (great yu) share the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Zuo-shang (left shang) and you-shang (right shang) share the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. Jia-gong (added gong) and da-gong (great gong) share the upper portion of the left Foot Shaoyang channel. Zhi-pan (substantive-split zhi) and da-gong (great gong) share the lower portion of the left Hand Taiyang channel. Pan-jiao (split jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the lower portion of the left Foot Shaoyang channel. Da-yu (great yu) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the upper portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Da-jiao (great jiao) and da-gong (great gong) share the upper portion of the right Foot Shaoyang channel. The subtypes of zhi are: you-zhi, shao-zhi, zhi-zhi, shang-zhi, and pan-zhi. The subtypes of jiao are: you-jiao, dai-jiao, shang-jiao, da-jiao, and pan-jiao. The subtypes of shang are: you-shang, shao-shang, dai-shang, shang-shang, and zuo-shang. The subtypes of gong are: shao-gong, shang-gong, da-gong, jia-gong, and zuo-jiaogong. The subtypes of yu are: zhong-yu, zhi-yu, shang-yu, da-yu, and shao-yu.

Huangdi said: 'Women have no beard — is this because they lack blood and qi?' Qibo said: 'The Penetrating Vessel (Chongmai) and the Conception Vessel (Renmai) both arise within the uterus, ascend along the interior of the back, and form the sea of the channels and network vessels. Their branch that floats to the exterior travels up along the abdomen, converges at the throat, and then divides to network around the lips and mouth. When blood and qi are abundant, they fill the skin and warm the flesh; when blood alone is abundant, it seeps out and moistens the skin, giving rise to fine hair. Now, in the constitution of women there is a surplus of qi but a deficiency of blood, because they repeatedly lose blood through menstruation; hence the vessels of the Penetrating and Conception channels cannot nourish the lips and mouth, and so no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'When a man is injured in his yin — when his yin qi is cut off so that he cannot become erect, and his yin organ is rendered useless — his beard nonetheless does not fall away. Why is this? And why is it that eunuchs alone lose their beard? I wish to hear the reason.' Qibo said: 'In the eunuch, the ancestral sinew, the genital organ, has been removed, injuring the Penetrating Vessel; the blood that is drained away cannot be restored, the skin binds up internally, and the lips and mouth are no longer nourished — hence no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'There are also natural eunuchs, who have never been injured and have never lost blood, and yet their beard does not grow. Why is this?' Qibo said: 'This is a congenital insufficiency bestowed by Heaven. In such persons the Conception and Penetrating vessels are not abundant, and the ancestral sinew fails to develop fully; they have qi but no blood, and so the lips and mouth are not nourished, and no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'Excellent indeed! The sage's comprehension of the myriad things is like the light and shadow cast by the sun and moon, like the resonance struck from a drum by sound — one hears the tone and knows the shape. Were it not for you, Master, who could clarify the essence of the myriad things?' Thus the sage, observing a person's complexion, knows that one who is yellow or red has abundant heat qi; one who is green-blue or white has scant heat qi; one who is dark has abundant blood but scant qi. One with fine eyebrows has abundant blood in the Taiyang; one with connecting whiskers and a full beard has abundant blood in the Shaoyang; and one with a fine beard has abundant blood in the Yangming — this is simply how it naturally is.

Now, the constant measure for human beings is this: the Taiyang habitually has abundant blood and scant qi; the Shaoyang habitually has abundant qi and scant blood; the Yangming habitually has abundant blood and abundant qi; the Jueyin habitually has abundant qi and scant blood; the Shaoyin habitually has abundant blood and scant qi; and the Taiyin habitually has abundant blood and scant qi. This is the constant measure bestowed by Heaven.

title_en: The Origin of the Hundred Diseases
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'Now, the hundred diseases, in their beginning, all arise from wind and rain, cold and summer-heat, clear coolness and dampness, and joy and anger. When joy and anger are not moderated, they injure the viscera; when wind and rain assail the body, they injure the upper regions; when clear coolness and dampness assail the body, they injure the lower regions. The three regions are each injured by different categories of cause — I wish to hear how they converge.' Qibo said: 'The qi of the three regions each differ: some diseases arise from yin, others from yang. Allow me to explain the pattern. When joy and anger are not moderated, they injure the viscera, and when the viscera are injured, disease arises from yin. When clear coolness and dampness take advantage of a deficiency, disease arises from the lower region. When wind and rain take advantage of a deficiency, disease arises from the upper region. These are what is called the three regions. As for the excessive overflow of these pathogenic influences once they take hold, their manifestations cannot be exhausted by counting.'

Huangdi said: 'Indeed, I cannot count them all — this is precisely why I ask my former teacher; I wish to hear the whole of this Way.' Qibo said: 'Wind and rain, cold and heat, cannot by themselves harm a person unless there is a deficiency present; evil qi alone cannot injure the body. Thus, when a person suddenly encounters a fierce wind or violent rain and yet does not fall ill, it is because there is no deficiency present, and so the evil qi cannot injure him by itself. This must always depend upon a vacuous, thieving wind meeting with a body that is itself deficient — when the two deficiencies meet one another, the evil is then able to lodge in the body. Where two fullnesses meet one another, the flesh of most people remains firm and resistant. Whether the body is struck by vacuous, thieving evil depends on the season of Heaven together with the condition of the body itself; weighing deficiency against fullness together, serious disease is thereby formed. The qi of disease has a fixed lodging place, and it is named according to where it settles — above, below, in the center, or on the exterior — and thus is divided into three regions.'

Thus, when vacuous, thieving evil strikes a person, it begins in the skin. When the skin is lax, the interstices open; once open, the evil enters through the body hair, and once entered, it presses inward and deepens; as it deepens, the body hair stands erect, and when the hair stands erect there is a chilled, shivering sensation, and so the skin becomes painful. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the network vessels; while in the network vessels, there is pain in the flesh and muscles, so that the pain comes and goes intermittently. If it is not expelled, the great channels then take over as its lodging place; if it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the channels; while in the channels, one shivers with cold and is easily startled. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges at the transport points; while at the transport points, the six channels fail to communicate with the four limbs, so the joints of the limbs ache and the lower back and spine become stiff. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the Hidden-Chong Vessel; while in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, the body feels heavy and aches all over. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the intestines and stomach; while in the intestines and stomach, there is rumbling and distension of the abdomen. Where cold predominates, there is intestinal rumbling with diarrhea of undigested food and failure to digest food; where heat predominates, there is sloppy, mushy stool. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges outside the intestines and stomach, in the space of the membrane-source, the fatty membranes, where it settles into the vessels; if it remains there without departing, it accumulates over time and forms a mass. It may settle in the grandchild vessels, or in the network vessels, or in the channel vessels, or at the transport points, or in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, or in the sinews alongside the spine, or in the membrane-source between the intestines and stomach, extending upward to connect with the slack sinews. The excessive overflow of evil qi in this way cannot be exhaustively described.

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear fully how each of these comes about.' Qibo said: 'When the evil settles in the grandchild vessels and forms a mass, the mass moves back and forth, upward and downward, for the grandchild vessels are slight and shallow, floating and lax, unable to grip and hold the mass in place; hence it travels back and forth between the intestines and stomach. Water gathers and seeps in, pouring and irrigating, producing a sloshing sound; where cold is present, there is bloated fullness with rumbling that radiates through the abdomen, and so there is intermittent, sharp pain. When it settles in the Yangming channel, it lodges beside the navel; when the person eats to fullness, it grows larger, and when hungry, it grows smaller. When it settles in the slack sinews, it resembles a mass of the Yangming, so that eating to fullness brings pain and hunger brings ease. When it settles in the membrane-source between the intestines and stomach, there is pain that extends outward to connect with the slack sinews; eating to fullness brings ease, and hunger brings pain. When it settles in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, palpation reveals it pulsing beneath the hand, and when the hand is released, hot qi descends into both thighs as though hot water were being poured over them. When it settles in the sinews alongside the spine, behind the intestines, the mass becomes apparent when hungry and disappears when full, so that it cannot be found on palpation. When it settles at the transport points, there is blockage and obstruction so that fluids fail to descend, and the body's orifices become dry and congested. This is how evil qi passes from the exterior into the interior, and travels from above to below.'

Huangdi said: 'From the very beginning of a mass's formation to its full development, how does this occur?' Qibo said: 'A mass, at its beginning, arises from exposure to cold; through reversal of qi it becomes a fully formed mass.' Huangdi said: 'How does it become a mass?' Qibo said: 'Reversal qi first produces a distressed feeling in the feet; this distress produces coldness in the shins; coldness in the shins causes the blood vessels to congeal and become rough; when the blood vessels congeal and become rough, cold qi ascends and enters the intestines and stomach; once it enters the intestines and stomach, there is bloated distension; and this bloated distension causes the fluid and froth outside the intestines to be forced together and unable to disperse, so that over time a mass is formed. If a person suddenly eats and drinks to excess, the intestines become full; if rising and resting are not regulated, and if one exerts oneself with excessive force, the network vessels become injured. When the yang network vessels are injured, blood overflows to the exterior, and overflowing blood causes nosebleed; when the yin network vessels are injured, blood overflows internally, and this causes bleeding from the stool. When the network vessels of the intestines and stomach are injured, blood overflows outside the intestines; if there is cold outside the intestines, the fluid and froth contend with the blood there, and the two combine and congeal together, unable to disperse, and a mass is thereby formed. If a person is suddenly struck by cold from without, or is internally injured by worry and anger, the qi rebels upward; when qi rebels upward, the six transport points fail to communicate, warming qi cannot circulate, congealed blood accumulates within and cannot disperse, and bodily fluids become rough and seep out, settling in place without departing — and thus a mass is fully formed in every case.'

Huangdi said: 'What of disease that arises from yin?' Qibo said: 'Worry and excessive contemplation injure the heart; doubled exposure to cold injures the lung; rage and anger injure the liver; entering the bedchamber while intoxicated, and sweating while exposed to wind, injures the spleen; excessive physical exertion, or entering the bedchamber and sweating profusely thereafter, injures the kidney. These are the diseases produced within the three regions, both internal and external.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one skillfully treat these conditions?' Qibo answered: 'Examine the location of the pain in order to know its correspondence, and determine whether there is surplus or deficiency. Where supplementation is called for, supplement; where drainage is called for, drain; and never act contrary to the proper timing of Heaven — this is what is called the perfection of treatment.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have learned of the nine needles from you, Master, and have put them into practice among the common people. Yet the blood and qi of the common people take differing forms: in some, the spirit stirs and the qi moves ahead of the needle itself; in some, the qi and the needle arrive together; in some, once the needle is withdrawn, the qi continues to move on its own; in some, only after repeated needling is the response felt; in some, upon inserting the needle the qi rebels; and in some, repeated needling only worsens the disease. These six patterns each take a different form — I wish to hear the explanation for them.'

Qibo said: 'In a person of doubled yang, the spirit is easily stirred and the qi easily moves forth.' Huangdi said: 'What is meant by a person of doubled yang?' Qibo said: 'A person of doubled yang is fiery and expansive in manner, quick and animated in speech, and lifts his feet high when walking; the qi of his heart and lung viscera is in surplus, and his yang qi is smooth, abundant, and outwardly manifest — hence his spirit stirs and his qi moves ahead of the needle.'

Huangdi said: 'Why is it that in some persons of doubled yang, the spirit does not move ahead of the needle?' Qibo said: 'Such a person still has a considerable measure of yin within him.' Huangdi said: 'How can one know that he has a considerable measure of yin?' Qibo said: 'One with abundant yang is much given to joy; one with abundant yin is much given to anger, though such frequent anger is also easily dispelled — hence it is said that he has a considerable measure of yin. Since the separation and union of his yin and yang are difficult to resolve, his spirit is unable to move ahead of the needle.'

Huangdi said: 'What of the case where the qi and the needle arrive together?' Qibo said: 'In such a person, yin and yang are harmonious and well regulated, and blood and qi are moist and smoothly flowing; hence when the needle enters, the qi issues forth at once, and the two meet swiftly together.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the needle, once withdrawn, to be followed by the qi moving on its own?' Qibo said: 'In such a person, yin qi is abundant and yang qi scant; the yin qi lies deep while the yang qi, though floating, remains stored within — hence, once the needle is withdrawn, the qi follows after it, moving on its own.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the response to be felt only after repeated needling?' Qibo said: 'Such a person has abundant yin and scant yang; his qi lies deep and moves forth only with difficulty, hence the response is felt only after repeated needling.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the qi to rebel when the needle enters?' Qibo said: 'Where the qi rebels, and where repeated needling only worsens the disease, this is not due to the disposition of yin and yang qi, nor to whether they lie deep or float near the surface — in every such case, the fault lies with the unskilled practitioner and the error of poor technique; the patient's own bodily form and qi are not at fault.'

Huangdi said: 'When qi causes upper-diaphragm obstruction, food and drink, once swallowed, are returned and vomited back up — this I already understand. But when parasites cause lower-diaphragm obstruction, the food is retained for a time and only later expelled — I have not yet grasped the meaning of this, and I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo said: 'When joy and anger are not properly regulated, when eating and drinking are not moderated, and when cold and warmth are not kept in their proper season, cold fluid flows into the intestines. Once it flows into the intestines, the parasites there become chilled; once chilled, they gather and accumulate, taking up residence at the lower portion of the stomach duct. This causes the intestines and stomach to become distended and full, the defensive qi fails to circulate properly there, and evil qi comes to lodge in that place. When a person eats, the parasites rise up to feed on the food; when the parasites rise up to feed, the lower stomach duct becomes empty, and when it is empty, evil qi overcomes it there, accumulating and lingering. Once it lingers, an abscess forms, and once the abscess forms, the lower stomach duct becomes constricted. Where the abscess lies within the duct itself, the pain, though close at hand, lies deep; where the abscess lies outside the duct, it protrudes outward and the pain lies near the surface, with the skin over the abscess feeling hot.'

Huangdi said: 'How should one needle this condition?' Qibo said: 'Press gently upon the abscess and observe the direction in which the qi moves. First needle shallowly at its periphery, then gradually insert the needle deeper, pursuing the abscess with successive needlings, but not exceeding three rounds of insertion. Examine whether the qi lies deep or near the surface, and let this determine the depth of needling. After needling, one must always apply a warming compress, so as to let the heat penetrate within; by causing the heat to enter day after day, the evil qi will gradually decline, and the great abscess will eventually break open and drain. Combine this with the appropriate dietary prohibitions in order to clear the interior; keep the patient tranquil and free of exertion, so that the qi may be made to circulate properly. Afterward, use salty and bitter flavors to help transform the grain so that it may descend properly.'
